patches_submitted.subject: [RFC PATCH] mm: bypass swap readahead for zswap

version_history.subject: [RFC PATCH] mm: bypass swap readahead for zswap

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch bypasses swap readahead for zswap, an in-memory compression backend similar to zram. The current implementation of swap readahead is optimized for traditional disk-based swap devices and can cause issues like inflated anon residency, page cache thrashing, and increased fault latency when applied to an in-memory backend. By skipping readahead for zswap, the patch aims to improve performance by reducing unnecessary prefetching of pages that are not needed. The results from testing this bypass show significant improvements in build time, swap-in/out rates, and reduced swap readahead hits.

review_comments.author: Kairui Song
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that bypassing swap readahead for zswap is an interesting idea but did not provide any specific technical feedback or concerns.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Alexandre

Thanks for the test and patch, very interesting idea.

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary: The reviewer suggested moving the bypass logic for zswap readahead into a separate, named function within the swap code to improve abstraction and maintainability.
review_comments.raw_body:
This should really be abstracted into a reasonably-named helper that can live in
swap code.

-- 
Cheers,

David

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Nhat Pham agreed with the patch, suggesting an additional improvement by calling xa_empty() to further optimize zswap performance.
review_comments.raw_body:
+1.

Maybe also xa_empty() when we're at it? :)

review_comments.author: Yosry Ahmed
review_comments.summary: The reviewer questioned the assumption that if a swap entry is in zswap, then all subsequent readahead entries will also be in zswap, pointing out that it's possible for remaining entries to be on disk instead.
review_comments.raw_body:
This assumes that if the swap entry is in zswap, then the remaining
entries (covered by the readahead window) will also be in zswap,
right? While not very likely, it's possible that the remaining entries
not in zswap but on disk, right?

review_comments.author: Barry Song
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Barry Song noted that readahead swap entries in zswap can lead to unnecessary decompression and additional page faults, and suggested a proof-of-concept patch to enable fault-around-like behavior for do_swap_page() when the readahead folios are in zswap.

review_comments.author: Alexandre Ghiti (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that the swapoff path needs to drop the per-vswap spinlock before calling try_to_unmap(), agreed to restructure in v2.

Author acknowledged a missing detail and agreed to include it in version 2 of the patch.
sentiment_signals: agreed to include it in v2
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Kairui,

On 6/24/26 12:30, Kairui Song wrote:

---

You're right, I'll add that in the v2.

Thanks,

Alex

review_comments.author: Alexandre Ghiti (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that the swapoff path needs to drop the per-vswap spinlock before calling try_to_unmap(), agreed to restructure in v2.

Author acknowledged the reviewer's suggestion to bypass swap readahead for zswap and agreed to implement a fix in a future version (v2).
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi David,

On 6/24/26 16:58, David Hildenbrand (Arm) wrote:

---

Makes sense, I'll come up with something.

Thanks,

Alex

review_comments.author: Alexandre Ghiti (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that the swapoff path needs to drop the per-vswap spinlock before calling try_to_unmap(), agreed to restructure in v2.

Author acknowledged that readahead should only occur for swap disk entries, not zswap, and is willing to experiment with modifying the readahead window.

Author agreed to rename the function from a proposed name to either another suggested name or zswap_is_entry_present(), indicating a fix is planned.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Yosry,

On 6/24/26 20:01, Yosry Ahmed wrote:

---

Yes, I assumed locality here.

Indeed, it would be interesting to keep the readahead but only actually 
readahead swap disk entries. I don't know how this will affect the 
readahead window (it is computed from the number of PG_readahead hits 
iirc) but I can give it a try.

---

Agree, the naming is not perfect, I'll change that to either your 
proposal or zswap_is_entry_present() (or something else), but I'll 
definitely change that.

Thanks,

Alex

review_comments.author: Alexandre Ghiti (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that the swapoff path needs to drop the per-vswap spinlock before calling try_to_unmap(), agreed to restructure in v2.

Author agreed to add a missing function declaration in zswap.h, confirming the patch will need further revision.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Nhat,

On 6/24/26 19:43, Nhat Pham wrote:

---

Yes I'll add that too.

Thanks,

Alex

review_comments.author: Alexandre Ghiti (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that the swapoff path needs to drop the per-vswap spinlock before calling try_to_unmap(), agreed to restructure in v2.

Author acknowledged a potential optimization for zswap by avoiding a second page fault due to synchronous readahead, no fix planned

Author acknowledged a potential inefficiency in zswap's readahead handling and suggested reordering the mapping of folios to coincide with the end of I/O operations, potentially moving it to an I/O callback.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Barry,

On 6/24/26 21:24, Barry Song wrote:

---

Oh I had not noticed that, indeed since zswap readahead is synchronous, 
we can clearly avoid the second page fault!

---

IIUC the code below, you wait for the end of the page fault to try and 
map a folio that would have been readahead right? I guess you do that at 
the end in the hope that the io has finished by then?

Maybe we can do that synchronously for zswap since the readahead is 
synchronous? And for the readahead pages that require io, wouldn't it 
be possible to do it in the end of io callback instead?

Thanks for your comments,

Alex

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Nhat Pham expressed enthusiasm for exploring zswap optimizations and encouraged further investigation into potential improvements.

Reviewer Nhat Pham suggested modifying the readahead logic to skip zswap and sync-io devices at each slot in the readahead window, allowing IO work to be submitted while sparing synchronous decompression work that might affect latency.

Reviewer Nhat Pham questioned the performance benefits of bypassing swap readahead for zswap, pointing out that if the pages are needed, it transfers work from future page faults to the current one, and if they're not needed, it wastes cycles.
review_comments.raw_body:
You're playing with zswap? Nice :)

It does feel like we're leaving some obvious wins on the table with
zswap here, so any ideas are much appreciated :)

---

Yeah I can see the edge case you and Yosry brought up here. As we move
towards supporting multiple swap backends in the same system, that can
certainly happen. I'm hoping that locality will bail us out - pages
close together in virtual address space hopefully have similar
lifetime, access temperature, compressibility, and will go to the same
swap backend, etc. - but who knows :)

What if we still follow the readahead logic, but at each slot in the
readahead window, we check if the entry is owned by zswap or is of a
sync-io devices, in which case we skip the readahead...?

That way, we will still submit the IO work, but spare the ones that
require synchronous decompression work, which might affect latency...?

---

Hmm so what your proposal buys us is, if we're already doing readahead
(synchronously), might as well install the pages in the window into
the page tables? :)

Could you explain to me how does this improve performance? If the
pages are needed, we are transferring some work from future page
faults to the current page fault. If the pages are not needed, then we
are just wasting cycles right?

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 5/5] btrfs: use GFP_NOWAIT when inhibiting eb writeback

version_history.subject: [PATCH 5/5] btrfs: use GFP_NOWAIT when inhibiting eb writeback

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the btrfs_inhibit_eb_writeback function to use GFP_NOWAIT instead of GFP_NOFS when allocating memory for inhibiting writeback on an extent buffer. This change is made because the allocation is already best effort and occurs while holding locks, making NOWAIT a suitable choice.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 4/5] btrfs: probe with GFP_NOWAIT for tree block readahead

version_history.subject: [PATCH 4/5] btrfs: probe with GFP_NOWAIT for tree block readahead

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the btrfs readahead mechanism to use GFP_NOWAIT when reading tree blocks, allowing it to fail instead of blocking in direct reclaim if allocation fails. This change is intended to prevent painful stalls and lock contention caused by readahead getting stuck in direct reclaim.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 3/5] btrfs: enable unlocked NOFAIL retry for eb allocations

version_history.subject: [PATCH 3/5] btrfs: enable unlocked NOFAIL retry for eb allocations

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch enables unlocked NOFAIL retry for eb allocations in the Btrfs file system. It introduces a new preallocation struct to carry allocation and signal requirements, allowing callers to attempt NOWAIT allocations if supported by the allocator. If the initial allocation fails, it signals that preallocation is required, enabling the caller to perform the necessary allocation and retry with the preallocated resources. The patch also notes that two GFP_NOFS allocations remain under lock and cannot be preallocated due to limitations in the filemap xarray.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 2/5] btrfs: add struct btrfs_eb_prealloc
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new structure called `struct btrfs_eb_prealloc` to carry the extent_buffer and btrfs_folio_state needed for NOFAIL allocations with retries outside the critical section. The existing allocation pathways are refactored to use this new struct, but no functional changes are made. Empty prealloc structs are wired in from callers. This is part of a larger series of patches to support NOFAIL allocations.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 1/5] btrfs: factor init_extent_buffer from __alloc_extent_buffer
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to refactor the initialization of extent buffers in Btrfs, allowing for preallocation and simplifying the code paths. The first patch factors out the initialization from the allocation function, setting the stage for future changes.

review_comments.author: Johannes Thumshirn
review_comments.summary: Raised a concern that the new init_extent_buffer() function is being used incorrectly in alloc_extent_buffer(), suggesting that either the old __alloc_extent_buffer() should be called or init_extent_buffer() should not be called.

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov
review_comments.summary: Clarified the intention behind the patch series, explaining that alloc_extent_buffer() should not call __alloc_extent_buffer() and that the new function is intended for future use.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/5] allocate extent_buffer GFP_NOFAIL with unlocked retry

version_history.subject: [PATCH 0/5] allocate extent_buffer GFP_NOFAIL with unlocked retry

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to reduce contention and stalls in btrfs by allowing extent_buffer allocations to be retried outside of critical sections using GFP_NOFAIL. The approach involves factoring out the initialization of extent_buffers from __alloc_extent_buffer, enabling unlocked NOFAIL retry for eb allocations, and probing with GFP_NOWAIT for tree block readahead.

review_comments.author: Johannes Thumshirn
review_comments.summary: Identified a potential inconsistency in how init_extent_buffer is used in alloc_extent_buffer(), suggesting that either the split out code should be removed or the call to __alloc_extent_buffer() should be kept.

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov
review_comments.summary: Clarified the intention behind factoring out init_extent_buffer from __alloc_extent_buffer(), explaining that it is a necessary intermediate step to eventually allow allocation to happen elsewhere.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v2] smp: Use release stores for csd_lock_record() state

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 0/9] dax/kmem: atomic whole-device hotplug via sysfs

version_history.subject: [PATCH v5 0/9] dax/kmem: atomic whole-device hotplug via sysfs

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 1/9] mm/memory: add memory_block_aligned_range() helper
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new helper function called `memory_block_aligned_range()` to the Linux kernel's memory management code. The function takes an input range and aligns its start address up and end address down to memory block boundaries, which is required for memory hotplug operations. This helper is added as a common function in `<linux/memory.h>` and is used by the `dax/kmem` driver to ensure that memory ranges are properly aligned. The patch also updates the `dax/kmem` code to use this new helper function instead of manually aligning the range. The goal of this patch is to simplify the process of handling memory hotplug operations and reduce the risk of errors due to misaligned ranges.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 2/9] mm/memory_hotplug: pass online_type to online_memory_block() via arg
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the `online_memory_block()` function to accept an online type via its argument, rather than calling a default function internally. This change prepares for callers to specify explicit online types.

review_comments.author: Gupta, Pankaj
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch with a Reviewed-by tag, indicating that it is technically sound and meets kernel coding standards.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 3/9] mm/memory_hotplug: export mhp_get_default_online_type
series_items.patch_summary: This patch exports the `mhp_get_default_online_type` function from the Linux kernel's memory hotplug module, allowing drivers to use it even when hotplug is disabled. The function returns a default online type of `MMOP_OFFLINE`, which is non-destructive and suitable for DAX use cases. This change enables drivers like CXL to build and use DAX without requiring hotplug as a predicate. The patch also adds an inline implementation for the function in case it's not enabled by CONFIG_MEMORY_HOTPLUG.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 4/9] mm/memory_hotplug: add __add_memory_driver_managed() with online_type arg
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new interface to the Linux kernel's memory hotplug functionality, allowing drivers to select their preferred online type (ZONE_NORMAL or ZONE_MOVABLE) when adding memory. The CXL driver is the primary motivator for this change, as it needs to plumb a preferred policy through the dax kmem driver. The patch refactors existing APIs and adds new helpers to support this feature, while ensuring that only known in-tree modules can override the system-wide onlining policy.

review_comments.author: Gupta, Pankaj
review_comments.summary: Gave Reviewed-by

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 5/9] mm/memory_hotplug: offline_and_remove_memory_ranges()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new function called offline_and_remove_memory_ranges() that allows callers to manage multiple contiguous memory ranges as one operation under a single lock. The function processes the ranges in two phases: first, it offlines every block of every range; second, it removes the ranges only if all ranges are offline. If any offline fails, the whole operation is reverted, providing atomicity and resolving potential race conditions between system daemons trying to manage the same resource.

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer David prefers the original code to be rewritten in a more readable format, specifically suggesting that the struct range initialization should be done directly instead of passing an array of ranges

Reviewer David noted that the function offline_and_remove_memory_ranges() will return an incorrect value when nr_ranges is zero, and suggested adding a WARN_ON_ONCE() to handle this case.

Reviewer David (Arm) suggested that instead of breaking and rolling back, the function should warn and continue, setting rc == 0 to fake a successful operation, as partially removing memory leaves no clean rollback path.
review_comments.raw_body:
I prefer this more readable as:

struct range range = {
	.start = start,
	.end = start + size - 1,
};

---

With "unsigned int" this will be !nr_ranges.

Wondering whether we would WARN_ON_ONCE() here.

---

Do we really want to break? I'd say, just warn and continue, and fake rc == 0.
Something is seriously messed up already, and we partially removed memory. There
is no clean rollback possible.

Similar to __remove_memory(), ignoring the error because it offlined it already.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged a concern about indentation in their code and is willing to adjust their writing style.

Author acknowledges the need to handle invalid input, suggests using EINVAL instead of a warning

Author acknowledged that the patch contained unnecessary code movement and agreed to revert the changes.

Author acknowledged that the patch needs to handle a specific edge case related to offline rollback and agreed to make changes in response.
review_comments.raw_body:
ack, ack.  need to reprogram my brain to two-indent style, i keep doing
this reflexively.

---

Seems reasonable.  Do we normally WARN when callers send dumb arguments?
Seems like sending -EINVAL is sufficient?

---

No, this was mostly my way ok keeping try of what was being moved around
while working it.  I will change it back.

---

This seems reasonable, will change to warn and continue + return error.

Sashiko actually pointed out there there's a corner condition here with
offline rollback, so i needed to tweak this chunk anyway.

~Gregory

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer David expressed mild concern about the error handling in offline_and_remove_memory_ranges(), suggesting that it should only be called when there's a strong expectation of no sane user doing so, but ultimately has no strong opinion and recommends keeping it as is.
review_comments.raw_body:
We do it when we really expect no sane user to do that and doing it would
indicate an actual problem.

Like passing a nid to add_memory_resource() that doesn't even exist.

But yeah, no strong opinion here, we can just keep it as is.

-- 
Cheers,

David

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 6/9] dax: plumb hotplug online_type through dax
series_items.patch_summary: This patch allows drivers that use the dax_kmem driver to specify an auto-online policy when creating a DAX device. It adds a new field, 'online_type', to the DAX device creation path, which can be used to override the system default online policy. The system default is still used if no explicit policy is specified, and this change does not introduce any behavioral changes for existing callers.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 7/9] dax/kmem: extract hotplug/hotremove helper functions
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the kmem probe and remove functions by extracting helper functions to manage memory hotplug operations. The new helpers, dax_kmem_init_resources, dax_kmem_cleanup_resources, dax_kmem_do_hotplug, and dax_kmem_do_hotremove, enable future extensions for more granular control over memory hotplug. This refactoring is necessary to support hot-add functionality by separating resource management from hotunplug/cleanup operations.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 8/9] dax/kmem: add sysfs interface for atomic whole-device hotplug
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a sysfs interface to enable atomic whole-device hotplug operations for DAX kmem devices. It introduces a new 'state' attribute that allows the device to be transitioned between different states, including unplugged, online, and offline. The patch also includes changes to handle legacy auto_online_blocks=offline policy and prevents deadlock during unbind operation. The atomic whole-device hotplug operation is achieved through a single call to dax_kmem_do_hotremove(), which collects all added ranges and offlines/removes them in one operation.

review_comments.author: Hannes Reinecke
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Hannes Reinecke questioned the need to treat 'unbind' as a given thing and suggested disallowing unbind when memory is online, recommending that admins offline the memory first using the new sysfs knob.
review_comments.raw_body:
That looks good, but question remains:

Why do we need to treat the 'unbind' call as a given thing?
If we know that we cannot handle online memory during unbind,
can't we just disallow unbind in that case?
I don't think it's too much to ask from an admin to offline
the memory first, _especially_ as now we have a simple knob
to do that ...

Cheers,

Hannes
-- 
Dr. Hannes Reinecke                  Kernel Storage Architect
hare@suse.de                                +49 911 74053 688
SUSE Software Solutions GmbH, Frankenstr. 146, 90461 Nrnberg
HRB 36809 (AG Nrnberg), GF: I. Totev, A. McDonald, W. Knoblich

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary: The author acknowledges that unbind is a violent operation and cannot fail, but still wants to prevent deadlocks by leaking resources if the device is online at unbind time. The author plans to reinstate legacy behavior when the state is not UNPLUGGED.
review_comments.raw_body:
No.  Unbind is a violent operation - unbinds cannot fail, and a
straight, uncoordinated unbind is essentially a `--force` flag:
the admin accepts the risks.

To your point, the admin either does the nice thing are they
muck up the system.

But we should still try to do something sane to defend the kernel,
in this case we should try to prevent that task from becoming
deadlocked.  The only way to do that is to leak the resources.

I'm making a small modification to this code to reinstate the
legacy behavior when "state!=UNPLUGGED".

~Gregory

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer David requested that the sysfs interface for atomic whole-device hotplug be split into a separate patch from the rest of the code changes.
review_comments.raw_body:
... and this as a separate patch, please?

Nothing else jumped at me.

-- 
Cheers,

David

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged David's feedback that the patch does not handle the case where a device is online at unbind, agreed to add a WARN in the cleanup routine and prevent hotremove if ->state is not DAX_KMEM_UNPLUGGED or MMOP_OFFLINE.
review_comments.raw_body:
ack

~Gregory

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 9/9] selftests/dax: add dax/kmem hotplug sysfs regression test
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a selftest to the Linux kernel's dax subsystem, specifically targeting the kmem hotplug sysfs interface. The test checks that the 'state' attribute of a kmem-backed dax device transitions correctly between unplugged, online, and online_movable states. It also verifies that force-unbinding does not deadlock and that invalid input is rejected. Additionally, it tests a specific regression scenario where re-onlineing after unplug must re-reserve per-range resources to ensure memory removal.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds atomic whole-device hotplug control to the dax kmem driver via a sysfs 'state' attribute, allowing for transitions across entire regions of memory to be performed atomically and safely. The current implementation has a race condition between offlining and removing memory blocks, which this patch aims to solve by introducing a new ABI with state names mirroring per-block memoryX/state attributes. The series also includes mm preparation changes to support the new hotplug control, including adding helpers for online type management and atomic offline+remove of multiple ranges. The dax/kmem feature is updated to plumb online_type through its device creation path and extract hotplug/hotremove into helper functions. A selftest is added to regression test the new attribute.

patches_reviewed.patch_summary: Discussion around error handling in memory hotplug patches.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Reviewer acknowledged the patch's reasonableness but questioned whether sending -EINVAL is sufficient for invalid arguments, suggesting a warning and error return instead.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Clarified that sending -EINVAL is sufficient for invalid arguments, but left the decision open to keep it as is.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v5 8/9] dax/kmem: add sysfs interface for atomic whole-device hotplug
Re: [PATCH v5 8/9] dax/kmem: add sysfs interface for atomic whole-device hotplug
